sanskrit_devanagari:
न हि प्रपश्यामि ममापनुद्याद्
यच्छोकमुच्छोषणमिन्द्रियाणाम् ।
अवाप्य भूमावसपत्नमृद्धं
राज्यं सुराणामपि चाधिपत्यम् ॥ ८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
na hi prapaśyāmi mamāpanudyād
yac chokam ucchoṣaṇam indriyāṇām |
avāpya bhūmāv asapatnam ṛddhaṁ
rājyaṁ surāṇām api cādhipatyam || 8 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.008 = MBh_06,024.008). Body text identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: I can find no means to drive away this grief which is drying up my senses. I will not be able to dispel it even if I win a prosperous, unrivaled kingdom on earth with sovereignty like the demigods in heaven.

I can find no means of driving away this anguish that is drying up my senses. Even if I win a prosperous and unrivalled kingdom on the earth, or gain sovereignty like the celestial gods, I will be unable to dispel this grief.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Although Arjuna was putting forward so many arguments based on knowledge of the principles of religion and moral codes, it appears that he was unable to solve his real problem without the help of the spiritual master, Lord Śrī Kṛṣṇa.

commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the explicit statement that no external acquisition (kingdom, sovereignty, even the rulership of the gods) can dissolve the grief Arjuna is in. The point is critical for the chapter: the remedy cannot be more of what the grief is reaching for; the remedy must be a different kind of thing entirely (knowledge of the imperishable, which the chapter will teach starting at 2.11).

Ramanuja stresses 'na hi prapaśyāmi' — 'I do not see' — the cognitive limit Arjuna has reached. He cannot perceive the remedy because the perception itself requires an instrument (knowledge of the self) he does not yet have. The verse is the disciple's report of his own diagnostic limit; it is the request for the teacher to supply the instrument.

positions.rendering: indicates the somatic intensity of the grief — it is not just emotional but physical

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All translators agree on the literal sense. The phrase is striking because it is somatic: Arjuna is reporting a body-state, not just a mood. The senses themselves feel desiccated. This matters for the chapter's diagnosis — the grief is not just a thought, it is a state of the operator's apparatus. The remedy must address the apparatus, not just the thought.

literal_meaning: Indeed, I do not see what may dispel this grief which is drying up my senses, even if I win an unrivalled prosperous kingdom on earth and the sovereignty of the gods.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Arjuna's explicit statement that no external acquisition can dispel his current state. The verse functions as the formal closure of the case Arjuna has been making since 2.4: he has run out of arguments, run out of imagined remedies, and now reports that even maximal external success would not fix what is wrong. The chapter takes this seriously. The remedy will not come from a different external configuration; it will come from a different kind of knowledge altogether (which the chapter begins teaching at 2.11). The verse is the formal handover from the disciple's last attempt at self-diagnosis to the teacher's first attempt at a real remedy.
